quizQuestion: What rotational analog of displacement measures the angle through which an object rotates, typically in radians?
explanation: Angular displacement Δθ = θ_f − θ_i, measured in radians (rad). One full rotation = 2π rad ≈ 6.28 rad. Like linear displacement, angular displacement is a vector (direction by right-hand rule for the axis). It is the rotational equivalent of Δx in linear kinematics.
visualDescription: A rotating disk with initial and final positions marked and the swept angle Δθ labeled

quizQuestion: What rotational analog of velocity measures the rate of change of angular displacement, in units of rad/s?
explanation: Angular velocity ω = Δθ/Δt, in rad/s. It is the rotational equivalent of linear velocity. A positive ω means counterclockwise rotation (by convention). Related to period: ω = 2π/T and frequency: ω = 2πf. Connects to linear speed: v = rω.
visualDescription: A rotating wheel with ω labeled as the rate of angular change in rad/s

quizQuestion: What rotational analog of acceleration measures the rate of change of angular velocity, in units of rad/s²?
explanation: Angular acceleration α = Δω/Δt, in rad/s². It is the rotational equivalent of linear acceleration. If α and ω are in the same direction, the object speeds up rotationally; if opposite, it slows down. Connects to tangential acceleration: a_t = rα.
visualDescription: A wheel with ω increasing over time, labeled with α = Δω/Δt in rad/s²

correctAnswer: Rotational equations mirror linear equations (θ↔x, ω↔v, α↔a)
acceptableAlternatives: angular kinematic equations have same form as linear equations
quizQuestion: How do the rotational kinematic equations for constant angular acceleration relate to the linear kinematic equations?
explanation: The rotational equations mirror the linear ones: ω = ω₀ + αt; θ = θ₀ + ω₀t + ½αt²; ω² = ω₀² + 2αΔθ. Each linear variable has a rotational analog (x→θ, v→ω, a→α). If you know linear kinematics, you know rotational kinematics — just substitute the angular variables. The same logic and math applies.
visualDescription: Side-by-side table showing linear and rotational kinematic equations with arrows connecting analogous quantities
distractors: Rotational equations only work for circles
The forms are similar but variables are inverted
Angular equations require mass in every term
Rotational kinematics doesn't apply to disks
Only ω = ω₀ + αt is analogous, rest differ
Rotational equations involve cross products always

acceptableAlternatives: tangential speed equals radius times angular velocity
quizQuestion: What equation connects the tangential (linear) speed v of a point on a rotating object to its angular velocity ω and distance r from the axis?
explanation: v = rω. Points farther from the rotation axis (larger r) move faster tangentially even with the same ω. This explains why the rim of a wheel moves faster than a point near the hub. Derived from arc length s = rθ, differentiated with respect to time: v = ds/dt = r(dθ/dt) = rω.
visualDescription: A rotating disk with two points at different radii, showing v₁ < v₂ for the same ω since r₁ < r₂

acceptableAlternatives: tangential acceleration equals radius times angular acceleration
quizQuestion: What equation relates the tangential linear acceleration of a point to the angular acceleration α and radius r?
explanation: a_t = rα. This is the component of linear acceleration that is tangential to the circular path (causes speed change). The other component is centripetal: a_c = v²/r = rω². Total linear acceleration: a = √(a_t² + a_c²). For uniform circular motion: α = 0, so a_t = 0, leaving only centripetal acceleration.
visualDescription: A rotating disk with a point on the rim showing tangential acceleration a_t = rα and centripetal acceleration a_c toward center

quizQuestion: What quantity is the rotational analog of force, measuring the tendency of a force to cause rotation about an axis?
explanation: Torque τ = rF sin θ, where r is the moment arm (distance from axis to force application point), F is the force, and θ is the angle between r and F. SI unit: N⋅m. Torque is what you apply when opening a door — same force applied farther from the hinge (larger r) produces greater torque. Also τ = r⊥F = rF⊥.
visualDescription: A door with force applied at various distances from the hinge, showing torque increasing with distance

acceptableAlternatives: torque equals r times F times sine of angle
quizQuestion: What equation gives the torque τ produced by force F applied at distance r from the axis, at angle θ?
explanation: τ = rF sin θ. Maximum torque when θ = 90° (force perpendicular to moment arm). Zero torque when θ = 0° or 180° (force along the moment arm — no tendency to rotate). Equivalently: τ = r⊥ × F (perpendicular distance from axis to the line of action of force) or τ = r × F⊥ (perpendicular component of force). SI unit: N⋅m.
visualDescription: A wrench with force F at angle θ to the handle of length r, with torque calculation shown

quizQuestion: What quantity is the rotational analog of mass, measuring how hard it is to change an object's angular velocity?
explanation: Rotational inertia (moment of inertia) I = Σm_i r_i² (sum of mass × distance² for all particles). Units: kg⋅m². More mass farther from the axis → larger I → harder to start/stop rotating. A solid disk has I = ½MR²; a thin ring has I = MR² (all mass at rim, so larger I). Figure skaters reduce I by pulling in their arms to spin faster.
visualDescription: A figure skater pulling in arms (smaller I, faster spin) vs. arms extended (larger I, slower spin)

acceptableAlternatives: sum of mass times radius squared
moment of inertia = sum of m times r squared
quizQuestion: What equation defines the moment of inertia I for a collection of point masses?
explanation: I = Σm_i r_i² sums the contribution of each particle: mass × (distance from axis)². Units: kg⋅m². The same total mass distributed farther from the axis gives larger I. For continuous objects, this becomes an integral. I depends on the chosen axis — the same object can have different I for different rotation axes.
visualDescription: Multiple masses at different distances from an axis, each labeled m_i and r_i, with I = Σm_i r_i² shown

acceptableAlternatives: net torque equals moment of inertia times angular acceleration
quizQuestion: What equation is the rotational analog of Newton's Second Law (F_net = ma)?
explanation: Στ = Iα is Newton's Second Law for rotation. Net torque causes angular acceleration, proportional to I (rotational inertia). Greater torque → greater α; greater I → smaller α for same torque. Just as F = ma uses mass as the linear inertia, Στ = Iα uses I as the rotational inertia. Units: N⋅m = (kg⋅m²)(rad/s²).
visualDescription: A rotating disk with applied torque τ and resulting angular acceleration α labeled, with Στ = Iα shown

quizQuestion: What condition must be satisfied for an object to be in rotational equilibrium (no angular acceleration)?
explanation: Rotational equilibrium requires Στ = 0 (net torque = 0). This means all clockwise torques equal all counterclockwise torques. An object in both translational (ΣF = 0) and rotational (Στ = 0) equilibrium is in static equilibrium. Seesaw problems, ladder problems, and hanging signs use this condition.
visualDescription: A seesaw balanced with a heavy child close to the center and a light child far from the center

acceptableAlternatives: perpendicular distance from axis to line of action of force
quizQuestion: What term describes the perpendicular distance from the rotation axis to the line of action of the force, which determines torque magnitude?
explanation: Moment arm (or lever arm) r⊥ is the perpendicular distance from the pivot to the line along which the force acts. Torque τ = F × r⊥. This is why applying a force far from the pivot (long moment arm) creates more torque. A force aimed directly at the pivot has moment arm = 0 and creates no torque.
visualDescription: A wrench with the perpendicular distance from the bolt to the force line labeled as the moment arm

correctAnswer: The distribution of mass relative to the rotation axis
acceptableAlternatives: how far mass is from the axis
mass farther from axis → larger I
quizQuestion: What factor, beyond total mass alone, determines an object's moment of inertia?
explanation: I = Σm_i r_i² shows that the SAME total mass can produce vastly different I depending on how far that mass is from the rotation axis. A solid sphere (I = 2/5 MR²) and a hollow sphere (I = 2/3 MR²) of the same mass and radius have different I. Redistributing mass to larger radii dramatically increases rotational inertia.
visualDescription: Two objects of equal mass — one compact (small I) and one spread out (large I) — both shown rotating
distractors: The speed of rotation determines I
Only the radius of the object determines I
Temperature of the object determines I

quizQuestion: What is the SI unit of torque?
explanation: Torque unit = N⋅m (newton-meter). This is the same as the unit of work (joules), but they are different physical quantities — torque is a vector related to rotation, while work is a scalar energy. To avoid confusion, torque is written as N⋅m (not joules). Torque = rF sin θ: meters × newtons × dimensionless = N⋅m.
visualDescription: A wrench applying 10 N at 0.3 m from the bolt: τ = 10 × 0.3 = 3 N⋅m

acceptableAlternatives: both net force and net torque equal zero
quizQuestion: What two conditions must BOTH be satisfied for an object to be in complete static equilibrium?
explanation: Static equilibrium requires both: (1) ΣF = 0 (no translational acceleration — object not accelerating linearly) AND (2) Στ = 0 (no rotational acceleration — object not spinning). Meeting only one condition isn't enough: an object could have zero net force but still rotate (couple of forces), or zero net torque but still translate.
visualDescription: A sign hanging from two ropes with force arrows showing ΣF = 0, and a balanced beam showing Στ = 0
distractors: All forces must be equal in magnitude
